fields.title: UNICEF Central African Republic Humanitarian Flash Update No. 1 (Cholera Outbreak Response), 6 July 2026
fields.body:
**Situation Update**

On 26 June 2026, the Ministry of Health and Population of the Central African Republic officially declared a cholera outbreak in the health districts of Bimbo and Mbaïki, following laboratory confirmation by the Institut Pasteur in Bangui.

As of 1 July 2026, active cholera transmission has been confirmed in three health districts, including the capital city: Bangui 2, Bimbo, and Mbaïki. A cumulative total of 214 suspected cases and 24 community deaths have been reported, resulting in a high case fatality rate of 11.21 per cent. Children under the age of 10 are disproportionately affected, accounting for 44 per cent of all reported cases. Males represent the majority of cases, with a male-to-female ratio of 1.21, corresponding to 117 male cases and 97 female cases. The epidemiological profile highlights the significant vulnerability of children and underscores the severity of the ongoing outbreak.

The outbreak continues to spread along the Ubangi River, affecting multiple communities across the impacted districts and increasing the risk of further geographical expansion. Given the high mobility of populations and the river’s role as a major transport corridor, there is a significant risk of transmission to additional localities and vulnerable communities along the river basin.

source.name: UN Children's Fund
source.longname: United Nations Children's Fund
source.spanish_name: Fondo de las Naciones Unidas para la Infancia

fields.title: UNICEF Ebola Virus Disease Outbreak Humanitarian Flash Update No. 1 (Preparedness and Readiness in Western Eastern and Southern Africa Region), 6 July 2026
fields.body:
**Highlights:**

- The Bundibugyo virus disease (BVD) outbreak remains confined to DRC and Uganda, with no confirmed cases reported in neighbouring countries to date. However, extensive cross-border movement, porous borders and humanitarian vulnerabilities continue to create a high risk of regional spread, underscoring the need to sustain readiness before the virus crosses borders.
- UNICEF is supporting governments in IPC/WASH, RCCE, emergency coordination and preparedness at priority points of entry. Countries are reinforcing frontline capacities through simulation exercises, pre- positioning of essential supplies, health worker training and community engagement to ensure any imported case can be detected timely, contained and responded to.
- UNICEF requires US$70.7 million to support the Regional Ebola efforts including US$ 29.1 million to support preparedness and readiness in the 10 high-risk countries. Significant funding gaps continue to constrain readiness and preparedness in those countries.

**HUMANITARIAN CONTEXT** **SITUATION OVERVIEW**

The Bundibugyo virus disease (BVD) outbreak in the Democratic Republic of the Congo (DRC), and Uganda, continues to pose a significant threat to the wider region. First confirmed in Ituri Province on 15 May 2026, the outbreak is unfolding in a highly complex humanitarian context characterized by insecurity, population displacement, fragile health services and intense cross-border mobility.

Recognizing the potential for rapid cross-border spread, the World Health Organization (WHO) declared the outbreak a Public Health Emergency of International Concern (PHEIC), while the Africa CDC designated it a Public Health Emergency of Continental Security (PHECS). In response, UNICEF activated its highest level of emergency response (Level 3 Corporate Emergency Procedures), enabling a rapid scale-up of outbreak response in affected countries.  
As of 29 June 2026, DRC has reported 1274 confirmed cases, including 360 confirmed deaths, while Uganda has confirmed 20 cases, including 2 death. Importantly, no confirmed Ebola cases have been reported outside DRC and Uganda to date. However, the risk of regional spread remains high due to porous borders, frequent population movements, trade corridors and refugee flows linking the affected countries with neighbouring States. Preserving this status requires sustained vigilance and continued investment in preparedness.

While response operations continue in DRC and Uganda, governments across neighbouring countries are transitioning to preparedness planning and operational readiness; strengthening the capacities required to rapidly detect, contain and respond to any imported case should the virus cross borders. With UNICEF's support, countries are reinforcing community surveillance, infection prevention and control (IPC), WASH, risk communication and community engagement (RCCE), laboratory capacity, coordination as well as preparedness at priority points of entry.

source.name: UN Children's Fund
source.longname: United Nations Children's Fund
source.spanish_name: Fondo de las Naciones Unidas para la Infancia

fields.title: République démocratique du Congo - Province de l'Ituri : Enquête sur la perception de la stabilité, les intentions et les vulnérabilités | Territoire de Mahagi (20 - 25 Juin 2026)
fields.body:
L’enquête menée dans 436 localités du territoire de Mahagi montre que 69 pour cent des localités sont perçues comme stables, malgré des défis persistants liés aux services de base et aux conditions de vie. L’eau potable demeure le principal besoin identifié (47%) et le service le plus souvent jugé insuffisant (93%), tandis que les moyens de subsistance et les soins de santé figurent également parmi les priorités majeures.

Les PDI, les personnes vivant avec un handicap et les femmes cheffes de ménage sont les groupes les plus vulnérables. Plus de la moitié des personnes déplacées (52%) souhaitent retourner dans leur zone d’origine, tandis que 29 pour cent envisagent une intégration dans leur communauté d’accueil. Les résultats soulignent la nécessité de renforcer l’accès aux services de base, aux moyens de subsistance et aux services psychosociaux, particulièrement dans les zones les plus vulnérables comme Angumu.

source.name: International Organization for Migration

fields.title: DRC Eastern DR Congo Ebola Situational Report #3 (6 July 2026)
fields.body:
The Ebola outbreak continues to expand across eastern DR Congo, with Ituri Province remaining its epicentre and sustained transmission also under way inNorth and South Kivu.  

The response has scaled up meaningfully over the past month, yet the outbreak is still outpacing the response: insecurity, access constraints, and community resistance continue to hamper operations on the ground. Contact tracing sits at roughly 82%;treatment centres are under significant strain; safe and dignified burials remain difficult to organise; and recurrent security incidents and border closures keep disrupting both access and logistics.  

The crisis is also spilling beyond DR Congo's borders. Uganda had confirmed 20 cases and two deaths as of 25 June, and on 24 June France reported an imported case, prompting several airlines to suspend DRC flights. In response, DR Congo's Minister of Health signed a decree on 24 June requiring anyone who has stayed in an affected province to complete a 21-day quarantine before travelling domestically or internationally - a sign that further restrictions are likely as the situation evolves.

source.name: Danish Refugee Council
source.longname: Danish Refugee Council

fields.title: Afrique de l’Ouest et centrale : Derniers événements en bref (30 juin - 6 juillet 2026)
fields.body:
**L’Afrique de l’Ouest**

*De violences inondations frappent l’Afrique de l’Ouest*

Selon des sources médiatiques, de graves inondations ont touché la Côte d’Ivoire, le Ghana, le Bénin, le Togo et le Nigeria, faisant des dizaines de morts et d'importants dégâts. Depuis le début de la saison des pluies à la mi-mai, au moins 59 personnes ont perdu la vie en Côte d’Ivoire, tandis que de récentes inondations ont fait au moins 13 morts au Ghana et cinq au Togo. Ces intempéries font suite à des pluies exceptionnelles. Le Ghana a enregistré plus de 140 mm de pluie en seulement 24 heures, l’un de ses plus importants cumuls journaliers depuis 1995. À Accra, la capitale, des quartiers entiers et de nombreuses routes ont été submergés, piégeant des habitants, certains contraints de nager dans une eau jusqu’au cou pour secourir leurs voisins. Des scènes similaires ont été observées à Cotonou, au Bénin, où des pluies torrentielles ont paralysé une partie de la ville. Les scientifiques alertent sur le fait que le changement climatique rend les épisodes de pluies extrêmes plus fréquents et plus intenses. Les gouvernements de la région ont annoncé des mesures d’urgence. Au Ghana, le président a ordonné la démolition des constructions érigées sur les voies d’écoulement des eaux et débloqué 29 millions de dollars pour les secours. En Côte d’Ivoire, les autorités poursuivent les opérations de recherche et appellent les habitants à quitter les zones à risque. Elles ont également annoncé la construction de 12 000 logements sociaux destinés à reloger environ 60 000 personnes. Selon l’Organisation météorologique mondiale, l’Afrique, bien qu’elle soit responsable d’une faible part des émissions mondiales de gaz à effet de serre, est l’une des régions les plus exposées aux effets du changement climatique.

**République Démocratique du Congo**

*Épidémie d’Ebola en hausse dans l’est sur fond de manque de financement*

Au 4 juillet, les autorités sanitaires ont recensé 1 561 cas confirmés d’Ebola dans les provinces de l’Ituri, du Nord-Kivu et du Sud-Kivu. L’Ituri demeure l’épicentre de l’épidémie, concentrant 90,8 % des cas confirmés. Les conditions insuffisantes en eau, hygiène et assainissement dans les sites de déplacement continuent d’alimenter les inquiétudes, avec au moins quatre sites où des cas ont été signalés. Les opérations de réponse se poursuivent, mais les acteurs humanitaires alertent sur de graves déficits de financement qui limitent l’ampleur des interventions. L’OCHA appelle à un renforcement des ressources afin de soutenir les interventions vitales dans 69 sites de déplacement en Ituri, où plus de 273 000 personnes déplacées font face à des besoins humanitaires critiques multisectoriels. Cameroun Recrudescence des violences et épidémie de choléra aggravent la crise humanitaire Les violences dans le Lac du Chad Basin se sont fortement intensifiées, avec une hausse de 80 % des incidents sécuritaires entre janvier 2024 et avril 2026 au Cameroun, au Tchad, au Niger et au Nigeria, a alerté le Haut-Commissariat des Nations Unies pour les réfugiés le 3 juillet. Dans l’Extrême-Nord du Cameroun, les attaques, enlèvements et l’insécurité persistante continuent d’entraîner de nouveaux déplacements. Près d’un million de personnes sont aujourd’hui déplacées, réfugiées ou retournées, tandis que 1,3 million ont besoin d’une assistance humanitaire. Les risques de protection s’aggravent. Un quart des communautés signalant la présence d’enfants séparés ou non accompagnés, tandis que les femmes et les filles sont davantage exposées aux violences dans un contexte de services de protection gravement sous-financés. La situation est encore aggravée par une épidémie de choléra confirmée le 23 juin, avec 123 cas et 10 décès recensés au 3 juillet, sur fond de craintes de transmission transfrontalière. Le financement humanitaire reste insuffisant, le plan de réponse humanitaire 2026 n’étant financé qu’à hauteur de 24 %.

source.name: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs
source.longname: United Nations Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs
source.spanish_name: Oficina de Coordinación de Asuntos Humanitarios

fields.title: West and Central Africa: Latest Events at a glance (30 June - 6 July 2026)
fields.body:
**West Africa**

*Deadly floods batter West Africa as extreme rains intensify*

According to media sources, severe rains and flooding have swept across Côte d’Ivoire, Ghana, Benin, Togo and Nigeria, leaving dozens dead and causing widespread destruction. Since the start of the rainy season in mid-May this year, at least 59 people have died in Côte d’Ivoire, while recent floods have killed at least 13 people in Ghana and five in Togo.   
  
The flooding follows exceptionally heavy rainfall, with Ghana recording more than 140 mm of rain in just 24 hours—one of its highest daily totals since 1995. Entire neighborhoods and roads in the capital, Accra, were submerged, leaving residents stranded and forcing some to swim through neck-deep water to rescue neighbors. Similar scenes unfolded in Benin, where torrential rains flooded large parts of the capital, Cotonou.   
  
Scientists warn that climate change is making extreme rainfall more frequent and more intense. Governments across the region have announced emergency measures. In Ghana, President John Mahama ordered the demolition of buildings constructed on waterways and released US$29 million for relief efforts. In Côte d’Ivoire, authorities warned the death toll could rise as rescue operations continue and urged residents to leave flood-prone areas. The government also announced plans to build 12,000 affordable homes to relocate around 60,000 people living in high-risk zones.

According to the World Meteorological Organization, Africa—despite contributing only a small share of global greenhouse gas emissions—is among the regions most vulnerable to climate extremes.

**Democratic Republic of the Congo**

*Ebola outbreak intensifies in the east amid funding gaps*

As of 4 July, health authorities have reported 1,561 confirmed Ebola cases across Ituri, North Kivu and South Kivu. Ituri Province remains the epicentre, accounting for 90.8% of all confirmed infections. Poor water, sanitation and hygiene conditions in displacement sites continue to fuel concerns, including at least four sites where Ebola cases have been detected. Response operations are ongoing in affected areas, but humanitarian partners warn that severe funding shortfalls are limiting their ability to respond at scale. OCHA is urging increased resources to support life-saving interventions across 69 displacement sites in Ituri, where over 273,000 displaced people are facing acute needs across multiple sectors.

**Cameroon**

*Rising violence and cholera outbreak deepen humanitarian crisis*

Violence across the Lake Chad Basin region has sharply intensified, with security incidents increasing by 80 per cent between January 2024 and April 2026 across Cameroon, Chad, Niger, and Nigeria, UN High Commissioner for Refugees warned on 3 July. In Cameroon’s Far North, ongoing attacks, abductions, and insecurity continue to drive displacement. Nearly one million people are now displaced, refugees or returnees, while 1.3 million require humanitarian assistance. Protection concerns are escalating, with reports that one in four communities includes separated or unaccompanied children, and women and girls facing heightened risks amid severely underfunded protection services. The situation is further compounded by a cholera outbreak confirmed on 23 June, with 123 cases and 10 deaths reported as of 3 July, amid concerns of cross-border transmission. Humanitarian funding remains critically low, with the 2026 response plan only 24 per cent funded.

source.name: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs
source.longname: United Nations Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs
source.spanish_name: Oficina de Coordinación de Asuntos Humanitarios

fields.title: IOM Multi-Country EVD Preparedness & Response Plan (Jun - Nov 2026)
fields.body:
1\. Regional Situation Analysis

The East, Horn and Southern Africa (EHSA) region is experiencing a significant cross-border public health threat following the declaration of the 17th Ebola virus disease (EVD) outbreak in the Democratic Republic of the Congo (DRC) on 15 May 2026, caused by the Bundibugyo virus. As of 27 June 2026, the outbreak had resulted in 1,274 confirmed cases and 360 deaths in DRC, concentrated in the eastern provinces of Ituri, North Kivu and South Kivu. Cross-border transmission has already been confirmed in Uganda, which had reported 20 confirmed cases and 2 confirmed deaths, along with 1 confirmed case in France as of the same date, with the outbreak epidemiologically linked to population movement from DRC. Neighbouring countries and those in the broader region, including Burundi, Rwanda, South Sudan, Ethiopia, Kenya, Angola, the United Republic of Tanzania, Zambia, the Central African Republic (CAR) and the Republic of Congo, remain at elevated risk of importation due to strong population mobility links, established cross-border trade and transit corridors and shared, porous borders with affected areas. Based on regional risk prioritization approaches aligned with WHO, Africa Centres for Disease Control and Prevention (Africa CDC) and national preparedness frameworks, countries have been categorized into priority groupings reflecting their level of risk and preparedness needs (including Priority 1 and Priority 2 contexts). Across these settings, preparedness measures have been intensified, including enhanced disease surveillance, traveller screening, infection prevention and control (IPC) readiness and cross-border coordination.

source.name: International Organization for Migration
source.longname: International Organization for Migration
source.spanish_name: Organización Internacional para las Migraciones

fields.title: DRC: Ebola BVD Outbreak: LTC Situation Report (Telecoms) #5 (Reporting period: 29 June - 05 July 2026)
fields.body:
*This situation report provides updates on the Ebola Bundibugyo Virus Disease (BVD) Outbreak in the Democratic Republic of the Congo (DRC). It outlines planned and ongoing telecommunications activities of the Logistics & Telecommunications Cluster1 (LTC) - Telecoms to support the ongoing humanitarian response.*

**Summary Points**

• Six weeks after the World Health Organization (WHO) declared the Ebola outbreak in the Democratic Republic of the Congo (DRC) a public health concern, confirmed cases have surpassed 1,400. This underscores the growing need for reliable connectivity at health facilities, laboratories and coordination hubs to support surveillance, data management, coordination and service delivery.

• LTC Telecoms supported UNDSS in repairing damaged radio repeaters in Bunia, restoring the dedicated UN security communications channel after six months downtime to restore and improve critical communications for humanitarian responders. This restoration is vital to humanitarians, especially health partners who are delivering services in Bunia, to safely conduct their missions.

• Following the 26 June field assessment in Mongbwalu, LTC Telecoms is initiating local procurement of a solar backup power system to sustain satellite connectivity at Mongbwalu General Hospital and reduce service disruptions caused by frequent power outages

source.name: Emergency Telecommunications Cluster
source.longname: Emergency Telecommunications Cluster

source.name: World Food Programme
source.longname: United Nations World Food Programme
source.spanish_name: Programa Mundial de Alimentos

fields.title: République démocratique du Congo : Cluster Sécurité alimentaire - Crise Ebola – sitrep #7 (Du 29 au 05 Juillet 26)
fields.body:
**Messages clés**

• L’épidémie d’Ebola continue à s’étendre dans des zones déjà confrontées à des niveaux élevés d’insécurité alimentaire.

• La couverture de l’assistance alimentaire reste insuffisante pour les cas confirmés, leurs familles et les contacts.

• Un positionnement rapide des partenaires et des financements flexibles sont nécessaires dans les zones de santé prioritaires.

**Qu'est-ce qui a changé cette semaine**

• A ce jour, on note 36 zones de santés affectées par la MVE

• Deux nouvelles zones touchées dans la province de l’Ituri à savoir Mandima et Lolwa. 67% des zones de santé de ces provinces sont actuellement touchées par la MVE.

• Parmi les zones de santé affectées, 8 d’entre elles sont classées en phase 4 avec environ 49% des personnes classées en IPC 3+

• Deux nouvelles zones de santé couvertes par les repas chauds à savoir Aru et Nia-Nia.

• L’épidémie d’Ebola a fortement déstabilisé les marchés, perturbant les importations et fragilisant les circuits d’approvisionnement internes. Cette situation, conjuguée à la flambée des prix, compromet l’accès à l’alimentation, en particulier pour les ménages les plus vulnérables.

fields.title: DR Congo Annual Report 2025 | NRC
fields.body:
In 2025, the Norwegian Refugee Council (NRC) reached 459,229 people across Ituri, North Kivu, and South Kivu with life-saving humanitarian assistance. Yet behind this achievement lies a stark reality: the Democratic Republic of the Congo has been ranked among the world's most neglected displacement crises for the tenth consecutive year.

As prolonged conflict, repeated displacement, and the deterioration of essential services continued to deepen humanitarian needs, NRC remained committed to delivering timely, principled, and needs-based assistance to communities affected by the crisis.

Through an integrated, multi-sectoral response, NRC provided support in education, protection, information, counselling and legal assistance, housing, land and property rights, shelter, water, sanitation and hygiene, and emergency cash assistance. These interventions helped meet urgent needs while strengthening the resilience of displaced and vulnerable communities.

The 2025 Annual Report demonstrates that flexible, predictable, and sustained humanitarian funding can deliver meaningful results, even in some of the world's most challenging operational environments. At the same time, it highlights a persistent gap between growing humanitarian needs and the resources available to address them.

As NRC looks ahead to 2026, the organization remains committed to expanding protection, improving equitable access to essential services, and advancing durable solutions for displaced people and host communities. Continued support from donors and partners will be critical to sustaining this impact and reaching those who remain in need.

source.name: Norwegian Refugee Council
source.longname: Norwegian Refugee Council

fields.title: Rapport de la situation epidemiologique du cholera en RD Congo (Interne OMS), semaine 25, 2026
fields.body:
**I. Points saillants de la semaine 25, 2026**

• Aucune nouvelle zone de santé n’a été affectée par des cas suspects de choléra à la semaine  
épidémiologique 25 de l’an 2026.

• Un total de 905 cas dont 15 décès (létalité de 1,7 %) ont été rapportés à la SE25 contre 939 cas dont 15 décès (létalité de 1,6 %) à la semaine 24.

• L’épidémie touche 14 provinces pour un total de 58 zones de santé ayant rapporté au moins un cas à la  
SE25.

• Au cours de la SE25, le Nord-Kivu (325 cas, 0 décès) et le Sud-Kivu (192 cas, 1 décès) demeurent les  
principaux foyers, représentant 57,13 % des cas suspects de choléra.

• Les provinces de l’Ouest concentrent 26 % de cas dont les plus affectées sont principalement le  
Kwango, Kongo Central, Maindombe, Sankuru et Equateur.

• Relance de la finalisation de la requête de la vaccination sur le corridor du fleuve Congo entre les deux  
Congo avec l'intégration des feedbacks de l'ICG.

source.name: World Health Organization
source.longname: World Health Organization
source.spanish_name: Organización Mundial de la Salud

fields.title: Chad | Floods - Operation Update #5 (MDRTD024)
fields.body:
**A. SITUATION ANALYSIS**

**Description of the crisis**

Over the past three years, Chad has been among the most vulnerable nations hit hard by weather extremes (droughts, floods) and lack of adaptive capacity, according to climate risk index data. In addition to the consequences of the 2024  
 floods, the populations of the affected areas are also suffering from the negative impacts of the floods in 2025 which  
 affected about 407,759 people and caused the death of 79 people in four provinces (Mayo-Kebbi East, Logone  
 Oriental, Moyen Chari and Tandjilé). These two floods, aggravated by persistent community conflicts, have displaced  
 many people, thus aggravating their vulnerabilities in the areas of food security and livelihoods, health, shelter,  
 hygiene, and sanitation. According to the 2026 seasonal forecasts produced by ACMAD (long-term forecast bulletin),  
 normal to probable excess rainfall is expected over most of central and southern Chad. The risk of intense rainfall  
 episodes between July and September increases, with a risk of localized flooding. Despite the efforts made by the CRT  
 to assist the most vulnerable, the gap remains significant in the field of health, disaster risk reduction and adaptation  
 to climate change. Repeated exposure to floods, displacement, loss of livelihoods, community tensions, and bereavement may also increase mental health and psychosocial support (MHPSS) needs among affected populations,  
 particularly among children, women, older adults, and displaced persons, highlighting the need for community-based  
 psychosocial support, strengthened social cohesion and resilience activities, capacity building of frontline staff and  
 volunteers e.g. in Psychological First Aid (PFA), and strengthened referral pathways for individuals requiring  
 specialized support.

source.name: International Federation of Red Cross and Red Crescent Societies
source.longname: International Federation of Red Cross and Red Crescent Societies
source.spanish_name: Federación Internacional de Sociedades de la Cruz Roja y de la Media Luna Roja

fields.title: Aumentan la violencia y el desplazamiento en la cuenca del lago Chad, advierte ACNUR
fields.body:
GINEBRA – ACNUR, la Agencia de la ONU para los Refugiados, está alarmada por el hecho de que la escalada de violencia en toda la cuenca del lago Chad esté provocando un fuerte aumento del desplazamiento forzado y de la inseguridad, lo que amenaza con revertir la frágil estabilización alcanzada recientemente en toda la región.

En toda la cuenca del lago Chad, que comprende amplias zonas de Camerún, Chad, Níger y Nigeria, más de 3,5 millones de personas se encuentran desplazadas por la fuerza, mientras que 8,2 millones necesitan asistencia humanitaria. La situación de seguridad se ha deteriorado drásticamente en los últimos tiempos, con un aumento del 80 por ciento en los incidentes registrados entre enero de 2024 y abril de 2026.

Solo entre septiembre de 2025 y mayo de 2026, se registraron casi 1.800 incidentes de seguridad y más de 5.700 víctimas mortales en toda la cuenca. Entre estos incidentes se incluyen ataques contra la población civil, asesinatos, secuestros, explosiones, enfrentamientos entre grupos armados e incursiones en aldeas.

El estado de Borno, al noreste de Nigeria, sigue siendo el epicentro de la crisis. Los repetidos ataques de grupos armados no estatales, las operaciones militares y la inseguridad a lo largo de las carreteras y las rutas de desplazamiento están desarraigando a las familias y limitando gravemente el acceso humanitario. Las repercusiones se extienden más allá del noreste, ya que los desplazamientos, la inseguridad y la competencia por los escasos recursos se extienden cada vez más a otras regiones, como el noroeste y el denominado ‘cinturón central’, lo que agrava las fragilidades. Desde enero de 2026, más de 77.500 personas se han visto desplazadas en los cuatro países, incluidas más de 16.000 personas refugiadas que huyeron de los ataques en el noreste de Nigeria y cruzaron recientemente a la región de Diffa, en Níger, donde ACNUR y sus socios están ayudando a identificar las necesidades urgentes, registrar a las personas recién llegadas, supervisar los riesgos de protección y proporcionar asistencia vital.

La violencia tiene cada vez más consecuencias transfronterizas, ya que los ataques en un país desencadenan rápidamente desplazamientos en otro. En el Extremo Norte de Camerún, los ataques persistentes, los secuestros y la violencia a nivel de aldea siguen provocando una inseguridad crónica y nuevos desplazamientos. En la provincia del Lago, en Chad, los ataques recurrentes y las operaciones militares han desplazado a unas 60.000 personas; en mayo se declaró el estado de emergencia tras un ataque contra instalaciones militares. Las personas desplazadas y las retornadas se encuentran ahora especialmente expuestas en zonas con servicios de protección limitados y pocas opciones seguras para desplazarse.

La población civil está sufriendo las peores consecuencias de la crisis. Un reciente monitoreo de protección en las zonas afectadas de la cuenca del lago Chad muestra que uno de cada cinco hogares afirma que ya no se siente seguro en su propia comunidad, lo que refleja el alcance de la inseguridad generalizada.

Las mujeres y las niñas se enfrentan a un riesgo cada vez mayor de violencia, mientras que los servicios especializados siguen adoleciendo de una grave falta de recursos. Según los datos, el número de personas que conocen a supervivientes de la violencia ha aumentado hasta el 27 por ciento en lo que va de 2026, frente al 19 por ciento de 2025, lo que indica un empeoramiento del entorno de protección en un contexto de importante subestimación, ya que las supervivientes dependen de redes de apoyo familiares y comunitarias que se encuentran al límite de su capacidad.

Niñas y niños están particularmente expuestos. Aproximadamente la mitad de las niñas y los niños de las zonas más afectadas no asisten a la escuela, porcentaje que se eleva a más del 78 por ciento en la provincia del Lago, en Chad. El seguimiento periódico de la protección muestra que una de cada cuatro personas informa de la presencia de niñas y niños separados de sus familias o no acompañados en sus comunidades, cifra que asciende a uno de cada tres en la región del Extremo Norte de Camerún. Los hogares monoparentales, las personas mayores y las personas con discapacidad también se enfrentan a riesgos elevados, ya que los desplazamientos repetidos erosionan unas redes de apoyo ya de por sí limitadas.

Las autoridades locales y nacionales de toda la región siguen desempeñando un papel fundamental para mantener abiertos los espacios de asilo y apoyar a las comunidades que se encuentran bajo presión. ACNUR colabora con ellas en los cuatro países para asistir a las personas que huyen de la violencia, supervisar los riesgos, apoyar a las personas recién llegadas y garantizar que las familias puedan acceder a documentación, asistencia y, cuando las condiciones lo permitan, a vías de retorno, reintegración y recuperación.

Sin embargo, la respuesta tiene dificultades para seguir el ritmo de la magnitud de las necesidades. [ACNUR y sus socios necesitan urgentemente 29 millones de dólares estadounidenses hasta diciembre de 2026](https://www.unhcr.org/africa/media/lake-chad-basin-regional-protection-and-displacement-crisis) para mantener las operaciones, garantizar la protección y la asistencia esenciales en las zonas de alto riesgo y respaldar los esfuerzos de estabilización regional liderados por los gobiernos.

Sin un apoyo oportuno y flexible, las lagunas en materia de protección se ampliarán, el desplazamiento seguirá extendiéndose más allá de las fronteras y aumentará el riesgo de que la crisis regional se agrave aún más. La evolución de la situación sigue siendo muy preocupante, pero aún puede revertirse si se brinda un apoyo sostenido desde ahora.

**Para más información sobre este tema, favor de contactar:**

source.name: UN High Commissioner for Refugees
source.longname: United Nations High Commissioner for Refugees
source.spanish_name: Oficina del Alto Comisionado de las Naciones Unidas para los Refugiados
